turns.text: Hello Brandi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. I guess… I’m just trying to make it to the next appointment without my heart racing out of my chest. It’s hard to put into words, really. Living out here in Leicester, the woods are so quiet, which is nice until it’s 2:00 AM and I’m staring at the ceiling, wondering if I’m going to make it through this. 

Last time, with the pre-eclampsia… it just happened so fast. One minute I was okay, and the next, everything went white and I was having seizures. I wake up in a cold sweat sometimes thinking about those hospital lights, just terrified that my body is going to betray me and my baby again. And then there’s the Hepatitis B, which feels like another thing I’m somehow failing at before he’s even here. 

The doctors are smart, I know that, but they just see a "high-risk" chart. They talk about numbers and protocols, but they don't see the long drive, the gas money I don't really have, or the fact that I’m doing this all on my own. It’s lonely. I want to be excited—I feel him kick, and it’s beautiful—but the fear is just always there, sitting right next to me. It's a lot to carry when you’re out here all by yourself.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

Honestly? I think I was naive. I think I expected someone to sit down with me, hold my hand, and maybe say, "Brandi, it’s going to be okay, we’ve got a plan for you." I guess I wanted someone to acknowledge that I was scared, especially after what happened last time. I wanted to feel like I wasn't just a project they had to manage.

But the reality? It’s just... clinical. It’s cold. You walk into that sterile room, and it’s like I’m already on an assembly line. They don't have time to ask how I’m managing the drive from Leicester or if I’m stressed about money. It’s all "blood pressure up a little," "what’s your viral load," "schedule the next scan." 

They’re efficient, don't get me wrong. They’re doing their jobs. But I feel like I’m under a microscope, and every time that cuff tightens, I’m waiting for the lecture. I wanted a partner in this, someone to help me feel brave enough to do it again, but mostly I just feel like a complication they’re trying to keep from exploding. I walk out of there feeling more isolated than when I walked in, just staring at the clock, dreading the next time I have to sit in that chair.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise? It was how quickly the warmth was replaced by the "high-risk" label. I walked in thinking maybe, just maybe, it would be different this time. I thought because they knew my history, they’d be more… gentle, I guess. 

Instead, it felt like the moment I stepped into that clinic, I stopped being a person and just became a list of risks. It wasn't even a conversation; it was a process of checking boxes. I remember the doctor looking at my chart and basically skipping over "How are you feeling, Brandi?" to dive straight into the medical protocols for my Hepatitis B and the monitoring for my blood pressure. It felt like I was being processed through a machine. 

There wasn't a single question about how I was coping at home or if I had any support. That hit me hard. I’m carrying all this weight—the trauma, the isolation—and it was like they couldn't see past the medical data. It made me realize that even if I *wanted* to tell them I was terrified, there wasn’t really any room for that in their schedule. They just wanted the data points. I left feeling so much smaller than when I arrived. It’s a really hollow feeling when the people supposed to keep you safe don't seem to see *you* at all.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I think I expected a conversation. You know, like in the movies? A doctor sits down, pulls up a chair, and explains things in a way that actually makes sense. I thought they’d say, "Here is what this means, here is what we are going to do together, and here is how we’ll keep you and the baby safe." I really, truly wanted to feel like a team.

But the reality is much faster and a lot more confusing. Most of the time, the results are just dumped on me in these short, frantic bursts. It’s either a quick phone call where someone reads off numbers I don't fully understand, or a rushed few minutes during an appointment while the doctor is already halfway out the door to see someone else. There’s no "we" in it—it’s just "your numbers are high" or "we need more tests." 

They use all these clinical words that just make my head spin. I’m sitting there, trying to translate their medical-speak into something that won't make me panic, but I’m too embarrassed to ask them to slow down. I feel like if I ask too many questions, I’ll be the "difficult" patient. So I just nod, try to hold it together until I get back to my car, and then I just sit there and cry because I’m still just as scared as I was before I walked in.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I guess I had this vision of a "primary" person—someone who would know my name before they looked at my chart, who remembered that I live way out in Leicester and that the drive is a struggle. I wanted that consistency, someone who saw the progression of my journey and knew *me*, not just my medical file. I thought I’d have one person who held the thread of my whole story, especially with the trauma I’ve been through.

But the reality? It’s a revolving door. I rarely see the same face twice. Every time I go in, it’s a different nurse, a different specialist, a different person asking me to recount the scariest moments of my life like it’s a grocery list. I have to explain about the eclampsia and the seizures over and over again, and I can see them glancing at the clock while I’m talking. 

There’s no continuity. I’m just starting from scratch every single time. It makes me feel so small, like I’m just a recurring headache for the hospital system rather than a woman trying to keep her baby alive. When you’re high-risk, you’re already drowning; you don’t need to be tossed between people who don't know you. It makes me feel even more alone in this.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It’s exhausting, honestly. Every time I see a new face, I feel like I have to put on this armor. I have to summarize my entire traumatic history—the seizures, the fear, the blood pressure spikes—just to get them up to speed. It’s like picking at a scab that never gets a chance to heal. By the time I finish explaining, they’re usually already rushing to the next step, and I’m just left feeling raw and drained.

Practically, it’s a mess. I feel like I’m constantly repeating information because it doesn't seem like anybody actually *reads* the notes from the last person. I worry that something important is going to slip through the cracks because there isn’t one person who has a bird's-eye view of my pregnancy. 

Emotionally? It makes me feel invisible. When you’re alone in the woods and fighting this level of fear, you need one person to look you in the eye and say, "I know your story, and I’m here for you." Instead, I get a different stranger every time who looks at the chart more than they look at me. It makes the whole experience feel impersonal and dangerous. I’m just a recurring entry in a computer system to them, not a mother-to-be who is fighting for her life and her child’s life. It just adds another layer of anxiety to an already terrifying situation.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I suppose I expected it to be about *me*—the whole me. I thought they would ask how I was feeling physically, if the fatigue was getting worse, or how I was managing the physical toll of living alone in such a remote spot. I expected that even with the medical stuff, there would be a sense of care for the vessel I am.

But the reality is that my body feels like a piece of faulty machinery to them. They don’t look at *me*; they look at my blood pressure cuff, my swelling, and my lab results. Every examination feels like a technical inspection. The way they poke and prod, the way they talk *about* my body while I’m lying right there, it’s all so disconnected. It’s like I’m a ghost in my own skin.

It feels invasive, not because of the physical touch, but because of the lack of humanity. There’s no "Are you comfortable?" or "I know this is hard." It’s just "Lie back," "Deep breath," "Hold still." I leave those appointments feeling objectified, like my body is just a failing project that needs to be brought into compliance. It makes me feel ashamed of my own pregnancy, like my body is the enemy that’s causing all these problems. It's incredibly lonely to have your own body treated like a problem to be solved rather than a home for my baby.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was this one appointment—I’ll never forget it. I was already feeling really swollen, and I was just exhausted from the drive. I’d had a rough night, barely slept, and I walked in feeling like I was barely holding it together. 

The doctor came in, didn’t even say hello, and immediately just started talking about my weight and the fluid retention. It wasn't "How are you feeling?" or "I see you're struggling." It was just this clinical, blunt assessment: "You’re retaining too much. We need to monitor this closer. Your numbers are trending in a bad direction." 

He didn't look me in the eyes. He just kept looking at the charts and then at my ankles, like he was looking at a broken part on a car. I felt so exposed. I wanted to tell him that I’m trying, that I’m doing everything right, but I was just so overwhelmed by the way he was talking about me like I wasn't even in the room. I felt this hot, stinging shame in my chest. 

I just stared at the wall, wishing I could be anywhere else. I felt like a failure. It’s hard enough to be scared for your life and your baby’s life, but when they talk about your body like it’s a source of trouble, it just makes you want to curl up and disappear. I didn’t say a word. I just nodded and waited to leave so I could finally cry in private.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I think I expected that managing a high-risk pregnancy would be a collaborative effort. I pictured a team—maybe a nurse or a counselor—who would walk me through the "how-to" of surviving it. I thought they would help me break down the overwhelming stuff into smaller, manageable pieces, especially given my history with the eclampsia. I imagined them helping me figure out how to handle the monitoring at home, maybe checking in to see if I had what I needed to actually pull this off safely.

The reality, though, is that it feels like I’ve been handed a heavy backpack full of rocks and told to climb a mountain alone. It’s all on me. There’s no support for the practical, day-to-day anxiety. I’m expected to manage these terrifying health markers in the middle of nowhere, and the only "guidance" I get is a list of strict protocols that feel impossible to follow when you're exhausted and broke.

It’s just pressure. They give me these instructions, but they don't give me the tools or the time to talk about why I’m struggling to follow them. It’s all about the *what*—what to track, what to avoid—and absolutely nothing about the *how* for a single mom living an hour away from help. It makes me feel like I’m constantly one mistake away from disaster, and I’m the only one standing guard. It’s not care; it’s a burden.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything was missing, really. I think the biggest gap is that they treat the pregnancy like it exists in a vacuum. They see the medical charts, but they don't see the life that’s actually happening around those numbers.

What I needed—what I still need—is just some acknowledgement of the reality I’m in. I needed someone to say, "Brandi, I know you’re terrified, and I know this is a long, lonely drive, so let’s talk about how we can make this work for you." I needed practical help, like knowing who to call if I feel those warning signs again, or even just some reassurance that I’m not a failure for having a body that struggles with this. 

It feels like they’re managing the disease, but they aren’t caring for the mother. They talk about "immediate neonatal vaccinations" and "viral loads" for my Hep B, but they don't talk to me like a person who is scared she won't survive the birth. I’m drowning in the fear of the future, and all I get is a brochure on pre-eclampsia. It’s like they’re worried about the baby’s safety but have completely forgotten that the baby’s safety is tied to mine. If I’m not okay—emotionally or physically—then how can I be the best for him? I just want to feel like someone is on my side.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I felt something was "off"—a headache that wouldn't go away, or a flicker of those old, terrifying symptoms—that I could just pick up the phone and get an answer. I thought there would be a nurse or a midwife who knew me, who would take me seriously because of my history. I imagined a safety net, really.

But the reality is a frustrating maze of automated menus and "leave a message" voicemails. When I’m sitting in my house in Leicester, feeling the distance from the hospital like a physical weight, that silence on the other end of the line is deafening. If I’m lucky, I get a call back hours later, but it’s rarely from someone who knows my chart. It’s some stranger asking me to explain my history from the beginning, which just makes my panic spike. 

It makes me feel like I have to decide, on my own, if my fear is "serious enough" to justify a trip to the hospital. If I drive all the way into the city and it’s nothing, I’ve wasted gas money I need for groceries. But if I stay home and I’m wrong? The thought is too dark to even finish. Being high-risk and isolated shouldn’t feel like I’m playing a game of medical Russian roulette, but that’s exactly what it feels like. The system isn't designed for someone like me; it’s designed for people who live five minutes from the ER and have someone sitting in the passenger seat. I’m just out here waiting for the next crisis, hoping I can get through the front door when it finally hits.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

There have been so many times where I just… sat on the edge of my bed, staring at my phone, trying to talk myself out of calling. 

I’ve had days where the swelling in my legs felt different, or I had that specific, sharp kind of headache that takes me right back to the eclampsia, but I just didn’t make the call. I’d tell myself, *'Brandi, don't be a bother. Don't be the hysterical, 'difficult' patient who shows up for nothing.'* I know how they look at me when I walk in—like I'm a waste of their time if the monitor doesn't show exactly what they expect. 

The thought of going all the way into the city, spending the money for gas, and then being sent home with a dismissive 'you’re fine, you’re just anxious' makes me feel so small. I’m already carrying so much shame about the high-risk label. I don’t want to be *that* woman—the one who’s always complaining. 

So, I wait. I wait until the fear is so sharp it’s unbearable. It’s like a terrible game of chance I’m playing with my life and my son’s life, and I hate myself for waiting. But when the system makes you feel like an inconvenience instead of a patient, the silence at home starts to feel safer than the judgment at the hospital. It shouldn't be that way, but it is.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I suppose I expected things to be different this time. I thought that maybe, just maybe, people would understand that being high-risk and alone is a serious thing. I had this idea that I’d have a friend to drive me to the big appointments, someone to check in on me when the nights got long, or even just someone to help with the heavy stuff around the house.

But the reality? It’s pretty quiet. Most people I know have their own lives—they’re working, raising kids, or just busy getting by. They don't really *get* what it’s like to be trapped in this "high-risk" bubble, constantly waiting for my blood pressure to spike or the seizures to start again. It’s hard to explain that kind of terror to someone who hasn't been through it. When I try to share, they just give me those platitudes like, "Oh, it'll be fine," or "Just try to relax." 

It makes me feel like a burden, so I stopped asking. I’ve retreated into the woods here, feeling like this pregnancy is a secret I’m keeping from the world—and from my own body. It’s isolating when you’re carrying a life, and the people around you don’t see the weight of it. I’m doing it all on my own, and sometimes, the silence in the house is so heavy, it’s all I can hear.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think I expected someone—anyone—to show up with a bit of "I’m in this with you." Maybe just a neighbor or a friend saying, "I’ll watch your place while you're at the hospital," or "I'll drive you today so you don't have to deal with the road." That kind of support never materialized. People around here, they’re all just barely hanging on themselves, trying to scrape by, so I think they view my pregnancy as something "too complicated" for them to touch.

The support that *did* show up wasn't really... helpful, I guess. It was more like unsolicited advice. People keep telling me, "Oh, just breathe," or "You're stressed for no reason, the baby will be fine." It’s meant to be kind, I know, but it feels so dismissive. It’s like they’re trying to silence my fear instead of hearing it. 

It makes me feel even more isolated. When you’re high-risk, you need people who will hold space for the terror, not people who want to sweep it under the rug. I’ve ended up feeling like I have to perform being "okay" for everyone else so they don't get uncomfortable. That is the opposite of the support I needed. I just wanted someone to acknowledge that this is hard, that I am scared, and that I’m not just being "difficult."

I guess it’s hard for people to understand that even when I’m surrounded by the woods—which look so peaceful from the road—the silence in my house is actually screaming. People tell me, "Oh, at least you’re in a quiet place, it’s good for your blood pressure." But they don’t see that when I’m lying there, feeling my heart beat hard against my ribs, that silence just makes me hyper-aware of every single ache in my body. 

It makes me feel like I’m waiting for a storm that I can see on the horizon, but I’m the only one standing outside without an umbrella. 

I think about the birth constantly. I’m so terrified of being alone when it happens, or that if I am alone, I won't know when things are going wrong until it’s too late—like last time. I keep playing it over in my mind: the drive to the hospital, the fear of the seizure returning, and the sheer, exhausting work of trying to stay calm for the baby. I want so badly to be brave, but it feels like the system and the isolation have worn all my bravery down to nothing. 

It’s just... it's a lot to hold onto by yourself. Every single day, I’m just trying to keep my blood pressure low and my spirits up, but I’m really just waiting for the day I have to face the hospital again. I’m hoping this time it’s different. I really, really hope it is. But right now? I feel like I'm just running out of air.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, all the time. It feels like my own intuition—the thing that’s literally keeping me and this baby alive—is seen as completely unreliable because I’m "the patient" with the "high-risk" label. 

There was this time when I told the doctor that a certain medication they had me on was making me feel like I was going to pass out, like my head was spinning and my heart was racing in a way that didn’t feel right. I told them I was really worried about it. And they just… they looked at my chart, waved their hand at me, and said, "It’s standard protocol, Brandi. Your body is just adjusting, and we need to keep the numbers steady." They didn't even look up from their screen. 

It’s like my lived experience, what I’m physically feeling in my own skin, doesn't matter unless it shows up on their machine. It’s so degrading. They treat me like a child who doesn't know her own body, or like I’m just making things up because I’m "anxious." 

It makes me feel like I’m fighting a war on two fronts: fighting my own health, and fighting to be taken seriously by the people who are supposedly there to help. When I’m told my own feelings don't count, I start to doubt myself, too. And when you’re already scared out of your mind, that self-doubt is the most dangerous thing of all. I just wanted someone to listen, not to tell me how I’m supposed to feel.

It’s just... I wonder, if I wasn't labeled "high-risk" or if I had someone with me—a partner, maybe, or someone who could actually advocate for me—would they listen differently? Would they stop looking at the screen and actually look at *me*? 

Sometimes I catch myself practicing what I’m going to say in the mirror before an appointment. I try to sound calm, clear, and "compliant," because I’ve learned that if I show any of this—this raw, shaking terror—they just write it off as "maternal anxiety." They don't see it as a logical reaction to having almost died before. They don't see that the anxiety *is* the symptom of the trauma. 

I’m so tired of performing. I’m tired of being the "good" patient who nods and says "okay" when they change my meds or tell me to come back in three days for another scan I can barely afford to drive to. Every time I walk out of that clinic, I feel like I’ve left a piece of myself behind in that waiting room. And then I have to get back in my car, drive back to that quiet house in the woods, and just... wait. 

I want to be excited about this baby. I want to buy the onesies and paint the walls. But right now, the fear of losing him, or not being here for him, is just taking up all the space in my heart. It’s like being in a long, dark tunnel, and I’m just trying to find the light, but the light feels like it’s miles away. And the scariest part? I don’t think anyone in that clinic knows how deep this tunnel actually is.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

Honestly, there isn’t a partner here to put pressure on me, and in some ways, I think that’s both my saving grace and the hardest part. I don’t have someone second-guessing my choices or pushing me to do things I’m not comfortable with, which is a relief. But it also means there’s no one to lean on when the weight of these medical decisions gets to be too much.

The pressure I feel is entirely from the "system." It’s like they expect me to rearrange my whole life—my work, my finances, my living situation—to accommodate *their* schedule and *their* protocols. They don't ask, "Can you make this appointment work?" They just tell me when to show up, like I don’t have a life, a job, or a house to keep running while I’m barely holding it together. 

I’ve had to make so many lifestyle changes—resting, eating specific ways, keeping my blood pressure down—but it’s all done under this cloud of fear that if I don’t hit their perfect metrics, I’m failing my son. I feel like I’m constantly negotiating with my own body, trying to force it to cooperate so the doctors will stop looking at me like I’m a problem to be fixed. It’s not a partnership; it’s an ultimatum. I’m just trying to keep my head above water, but the demands keep coming.

I look back at who I was before this second pregnancy, and I feel like I’ve aged a decade in just a few months. Sometimes I walk through the woods behind my place, and I try to imagine a version of this where I’m not constantly waiting for the "bad" news. I try to imagine a version where I’m not just a set of vitals on a screen, but a mother-to-be who’s actually allowed to be scared without being pathologized. 

The hardest thing, I think, is that I feel like I'm grieving a version of my pregnancy that I never got to have. I wanted that "glowing" experience people talk about—picking out tiny socks, decorating a nursery—but instead, I’m just navigating a minefield of clinical, cold, and impersonal interactions. I’m mourning the loss of the trust I *wanted* to have in my care team.

I’m really trying, you know? I’m going to the appointments, I’m following the protocols, and I’m taking the meds. But when I’m alone at night, I wonder: *Is all of this actually making it safer, or is it just making me lonelier?* I’m so worried that by the time I finally hold my baby, I’ll be so burnt out from the fear and the isolation that I won't even know how to just be a mom. I just want to breathe. I want to feel like, for once, someone is rooting for me, not just tracking my survival. It’s a heavy, heavy thing to hold.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

They act like my body is a crime scene they’re trying to solve. It’s almost every single time—some nurse or technician will mention my weight or my swelling like it’s a moral failing, as if I’m just choosing to be like this, like I’m not already doing everything I can to keep us both alive. 

It hits me so hard because I’m already so self-conscious. I look in the mirror and I don’t see a woman growing a life; I see a "high-risk patient" with "complications." When they make those little remarks—"Oh, we need to watch that weight gain," or "Your ankles are quite swollen today"—they don't hear how loud those words sound in my head. They don't know that I go home and measure every bite of food I eat, or that I spend hours with my legs propped up, crying because I’m so afraid my body is going to shut down again.

It makes me feel like I’m failing a test I never signed up to take. It’s not just an observation to them; it’s a judgment. It’s like they’re saying, "If you just did better, you wouldn’t be in this position." And that hurts more than the medical fear itself. It makes me feel small, ashamed, and completely untethered. I just want someone to look at me and say, "You're doing a brave thing," instead of nitpicking every inch of me like I'm a faulty machine.

You know, when I sit there, staring at their plastic-covered chairs and listening to them tick boxes on their forms, I sometimes wonder if they even realize that my body is a home for my son. They talk about "maternal weight" or "fluid retention" as if I’m just a vessel they’re monitoring for a crash. They don't see the love, the terror, the late-night talks I have with him while I’m lying awake in the dark. 

I’m so tired of the judgment. It’s like they think I want to be here. Like I want to be the woman who has to drive an hour just to be told that her body is "concerning" again. If I could be healthy, if I could just have a "normal" pregnancy, don't they think I would? 

Every time they make a comment—whether it’s about my weight or my numbers—it just reinforces that feeling that I’m not doing enough. That I’m the reason this is hard. And it’s so unfair. I am working harder than I’ve ever worked in my life. I’m holding down a job, I’m navigating the logistics of being a single mom in the middle of nowhere, and I’m doing it while fighting the ghost of my last delivery. 

I just want to be seen for the person I am, not the patient I’ve become. I want someone to ask me if I’m okay, and actually wait for the answer. Not the "I’m fine" answer I give them, but the real one—the one where I say I’m terrified and I don’t know if I can do this. But I guess that’s asking for too much in a fifteen-minute appointment, isn't it?
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

It’s embarrassing, but I think the first person I turn to is the internet. I know doctors hate it, and they always tell me to stay off the forums, but they don't get it. At 3:00 AM, when I’m feeling that familiar, sharp, stabbing headache and my vision is blurring, I don’t have a midwife on speed dial who actually *knows* me. I have Google, or these random online message boards where I search "pre-eclampsia symptoms" or "high-risk pregnancy alone."

I’m looking for someone who *gets it*. I’m looking for one other woman who’s been through that blinding white light of a seizure, or someone who’s balancing a job and a high-risk diagnosis while sitting in an empty house. I don’t want a protocol; I want someone to say, "I felt like that too, and I survived it." 

I’ve also started talking to a neighbor, just briefly, whenever I see her while getting the mail. She doesn't know the medical details, but just having someone acknowledge that I’m pregnant, that I’m *trying*, helps. She’s the only one who doesn’t treat me like I’m a complication. 

It’s sad, really—the fact that I trust an anonymous stranger on the internet more than the person in the white coat. But at least the stranger doesn't make me feel like I’m just a failure of a patient. They just listen. They don’t judge. It’s the only place I feel like I can take a breath.

Sometimes I feel like I'm living a double life. There’s the "compliant" Brandi who walks into the office, nods at the nurse, and answers the questions exactly how they want them answered so I don't get lectured. Then there’s the real Brandi, who is sitting at her kitchen table in the middle of the night, heart hammering, trying to find a post on some forum from three years ago that describes exactly what I'm feeling right now.

That’s where I get my comfort—from those strangers. I read their stories about the seizures, the hospital, the fear, and I realize I’m not just a freak occurrence. There are others. They talk about the "what-ifs" that the doctors refuse to acknowledge. When a doctor says, "We'll cross that bridge if we come to it," they’re trying to be professional, but to me, it sounds like they’re ignoring the fact that I’m standing right at the edge of the cliff. But those women online? They say, "I was at the edge, too, and here is how I survived." 

It’s not perfect, and I know it’s not "medically sound" advice, but it’s the only place where the conversation doesn't feel like a transaction. It’s the only place where I feel human. I wish my doctor could see that—that if they just gave me a little bit of that empathy, maybe I wouldn't have to look for it in the dark, digital corners of the world. It’s just so lonely, hoping for someone to be on your side, and finding that support in a screen instead of a person.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

Honestly? I can count on one hand the number of times I’ve felt like my input actually mattered, and even then, it felt like an illusion. Most of the time, the "plan" is already sitting on the counter when I walk into the room, printed out and waiting for my signature. It’s not a conversation; it’s a notification of what’s going to happen to my body next.

When they talk about induction dates or those extra tests for my Hep B, it’s always phrased as, "We’re going to do X, Y, and Z." It’s never, "How does your body feel about this?" or "Is this feasible for your life in Leicester?" I’m just expected to say "okay" and figure out how to make the driving or the downtime work. If I try to interject—like when I asked if we could schedule an appointment for a time when I wouldn't have to miss a full shift at work—the doctor just sighs, like I’m being unreasonable for worrying about my paycheck.

The only time I feel heard is when I’m talking to the receptionist about the parking or something trivial. The big stuff—the stuff that determines if I’m going to have a seizure or if my baby is going to be okay—that’s all on them. I’m just the passenger in my own pregnancy, and it makes me feel like I’ve lost all control. I just want them to realize that I’m the one living in this body, and I deserve a say in how it’s treated. But right now, I’m just being told what to do, and I’m too scared to push back.

It’s like being in a car where the driver isn't even looking at the road, they’re just following a GPS that doesn’t account for the potholes. They have their "standard of care" and their "clinical pathway," and if I don't fit perfectly into that, I’m just an inconvenience that slows down the process.

I remember once, trying to explain that the stress of the commute—the long, empty stretches of road where I’m terrified of my blood pressure spiking while I’m alone behind the wheel—was actually making me *more* symptomatic. I wanted to see if we could consolidate some of the appointments or maybe do a telehealth check-in for the routine stuff. The doctor didn't even look at me. She just adjusted her glasses and said, "We need you in-clinic for accurate readings, Brandi. It’s for your safety." 

Like I didn’t know it was for my safety? Like I’m somehow reckless with my own life? It felt so condescending. I wasn't asking to skip the care; I was asking to be treated like an adult who understands her own life. But the "plan" is the plan, and there’s no room for a human being in the margins of it. 

I feel like a ghost in those offices. I talk, but I’m not really heard. I’m just a data set that’s currently "high-risk." When you’re treated like that for months, you start to wonder if your voice actually matters at all. You start to think, "Maybe they’re right, maybe I should just stay quiet and let them tell me what to do." And that silence—the one where I stop advocating for myself—is exactly where the real danger starts. That’s what haunts me. It’s not just the illness; it’s the way they’ve stripped away my agency, piece by piece, until I’m just a vessel waiting for the next procedure.

I think what keeps me awake is the fear that if I don’t find my voice before the delivery, I’ll be completely silenced when I’m actually in the bed, in pain, and fighting for my life again. I know what that looks like—the white lights, the confusion, the feeling of being strapped down while people in scrubs make decisions *for* me. 

Last time, when the eclampsia hit, I didn't have a choice. I was gone, I wasn't there. But this time? I’m here. I’m conscious. I’m scared, but I’m here. And yet, every time I try to talk about that—about wanting a plan that keeps me awake, or about how terrified I am of feeling trapped again—they talk about "maternal safety" and "standard protocols." It’s like they think my trauma is just a pesky, emotional side effect, not a crucial part of my medical reality. 

I’m trying so hard to be "good" because I want to be safe, but I’m realizing that being "good" is just making me invisible. It’s like I have to scream just to be seen as a person, but if I scream, they just label me "anxious" and dismiss me even faster. It’s a trap, isn’t it? I just want to walk into that hospital, know who is going to be there, and feel like they’ve actually read my story. I want to feel like I’m a participant, not just the next case on their list. Is that really too much to ask?

Sometimes I sit in my car in that hospital parking lot, just staring at the building, and I think about the sheer amount of energy it takes just to walk through those doors. I have to put on this mask, this "compliant, high-risk patient" mask, because if I don't, I know the interaction will be even colder. If I admit that I’m drowning, that the stress of the travel, the work, and the memory of the seizures is a weight I can’t carry, they look at me like I’m a liability.

It feels like my pregnancy is a ticking clock, and the doctors are the ones setting the alarm. They aren’t asking me how to turn it off; they’re just measuring how fast it’s ticking. Every time I get a test back, I feel like I’m being graded. Am I passing? Am I failing? It’s not a medical relationship; it’s a high-stakes exam where the answer is either "safe" or "not safe." 

I just want to be treated like someone who has lived through a nightmare and is trying to come out the other side. I want them to look at the whole picture—not just my blood pressure or my viral load, but the woman who drives those miles alone every time. I want them to know that when I walk in, I’m bringing more than just a medical history; I’m bringing a heart that is so scared of the white lights that it’s hard to breathe. 

If they could just see that—if they could just acknowledge that I am a human being fighting for my life and my baby’s life—maybe I wouldn’t feel so incredibly lost. Maybe then, this path wouldn't feel like a walk to the gallows. I’m just one person, and I’m so, so tired of holding it all together by myself. I just want to know that I’m not alone in this, but the way they treat me, it’s like I’m the only one here.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every time I leave an appointment, I feel like I have this huge, heavy question mark hanging over me. They talk about "protocols" and "clinical pathways," but they don't tell me what that *feels* like. I go home and I type into Google, "Does anyone else have this weird, fluttering feeling in their chest when they’re on this blood pressure med?" or "How do you handle the panic when you're driving alone and you think you’re having an aura?"

I’m looking for the *human* side of the diagnosis. The doctors give me the statistics—the percentages of this or that happening—but they never tell me how to manage the fear of those statistics. I’m looking for someone to say, "Hey, I had that exact headache, and I took a cold shower and breathed through it," or "Here’s how I explained my needs to the nurse so they actually listened." 

I need practical, gritty advice for survival that isn't just "follow the protocol." I need to know how to navigate the system without losing my mind. The doctors are so guarded, so precise with their words, that I feel like if I ask something too "real" or too "emotional," I’ll be shut down. So I go online to find out how other women—real women, not patients in a chart—have managed to make it out the other side of a high-risk birth. I’m looking for hope, I guess, and a map that actually accounts for the potholes I’m hitting every single day.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that keeps spinning around in my head is that I want to be a mother—not just a case study for "successful management of a high-risk pregnancy." When I’m in that office, the conversation is always about the "outcome"—the delivery, the baby’s birth weight, the viral load levels—but they never talk about *me* as a mother. They don’t ask who I am, what my life is like, or if I have any joy left in me. 

I’m so terrified that when this is all over, I’ll look back and realize the only thing I remember is the fear and the hospital lights. I want to feel like someone cared about the transition, about the woman I’m becoming through all this trauma. 

Maybe the best way to help other women like me—women who are alone, who are scared, and who have to drive an hour just to be told they’re "high-risk"—is to remind the people in the white coats that they’re seeing a person, not a set of failing parts. Just a little humanity, a little softness, and maybe—just maybe—an acknowledgement that being a single mother doing this alone is a monumental, difficult thing. That’s all I’ve wanted from the start: to be seen, to be heard, and to know that I’m not just a ticking clock they’re waiting to go off. I just want to feel like a mother, not a patient.
